name: Common Lisp Incremental Redefinition
summary: Common Lisp treats the live image as the primary development environment: every function, class, method, and variable can be redefined at runtime. CLOS method dispatch tables propagate changes automatically. The ancestor of Julia Revise and Smalltalk-style live coding.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Common Lisp treats the live image as the primary development environment: every function, class, method, and variable can be redefined at runtime. `(defun foo () ...)` evaluated interactively replaces the existing `foo`; future calls see the new definition. Method dispatch tables (CLOS) propagate changes automatically; global variable rebinding takes effect immediately.

The SLIME / SLY IDE environments build on this to provide "compile-defun" and "compile-file" commands that update the running image incrementally. Combined with the condition/restart system, the workflow is: run code → hit an error → the debugger pops up → edit the offending function → restart the frame → continue.

sections.title: Image-based vs file-based
sections.content:
This development style — sometimes called "image-based" or "residential" — is philosophically distinct from edit-compile-run. The whole system is always live; code changes are made *to* the running system rather than to a file that will later be recompiled. Smalltalk pushed this idea further; Common Lisp established the compiled, static-type-free version that most modern interactive languages inherit.

The production lesson: image-based development requires the language and runtime to be co-designed for it. Function bindings must be rebindable, method dispatch must be late-binding, and the compiler must support recompilation of individual definitions. Languages that lock down these aspects (most static languages) cannot offer the same workflow.
